title: Rhubarb Fool
subtitle: Stewed rhubarb cooled and folded through whipped double cream
excerpt: Forced or outdoor rhubarb cooked with sugar to a thick, rosy compote, cooled completely, then folded through softly whipped double cream. Pink-streaked when folded, sharp against sweet. A British spring and early summer dessert that requires no equipment beyond a saucepan and a bowl.
sourceNotes: Rhubarb arrived in British cooking in the late seventeenth century and was initially used for medicinal purposes before its culinary value was established. Rhubarb fool became a standard British spring dessert as the Yorkshire forcing industry developed in the nineteenth century, producing the bright pink forced rhubarb available from January through March.

content.text: Forced rhubarb, the bright pink stalks grown in the dark in the Yorkshire forcing sheds from January through March, gives the most striking colour and the most delicate, sweet-sharp flavour. Outdoor rhubarb (April through July) is greener, more fibrous, and sharper, and needs a little more sugar. Both produce a good fool.

Let the compote collapse fully rather than keeping the rhubarb in pieces. A smooth, thick purée folds through the cream more evenly than chunks, which can sink and give an uneven result. Cool completely before folding, warm compote melts the cream.

items.name: Rhubarb
items.prepNote: trimmed and cut into 3 cm pieces

content.text: Put {{rhubarb}} of trimmed rhubarb into a medium saucepan with {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar and 2 tablespoons of water. Cook over medium heat, stirring occasionally, for 10, 15 minutes until the rhubarb has completely collapsed to a thick, bright pink compote. Stir in the vanilla extract if using. Taste and add more sugar if needed. Leave to cool completely, then chill in the fridge.

When the compote is cold, whip {{double-cream}} of double cream to soft peaks. Add the cold rhubarb compote and fold in gently, keeping some visible streaks of pink against the white cream. Spoon into glasses and chill for at least 1 hour before serving.

items.fix: The compote must be cold from the fridge. If it is very liquid, drain off some of the water before folding.
items.cause: Compote was warm when folded in, or had too much liquid

items.fix: Continue cooking with the lid on for another 5 minutes. Forced rhubarb collapses quickly; outdoor rhubarb may need 20 minutes total.
items.cause: Older outdoor rhubarb needs longer to collapse
items.symptom: Rhubarb is still in pieces after 15 minutes

items.fix: Add a little more sugar to the compote. Outdoor rhubarb from late in the season can be particularly sharp.
items.cause: Outdoor rhubarb, which is more acidic than forced

items.fix: Use a large spoon and fold in no more than 4, 5 movements. Some visible streaks of rhubarb against white cream look better and give more textural contrast.
items.symptom: Cream turned pink throughout rather than streaked

content.text: Rhubarb has been part of British cooking since the late seventeenth century. It came from China and was first used as a medicinal purgative before its kitchen use caught on. The forcing method (growing rhubarb in heated sheds in the dark to produce tender pink stalks in winter) was developed in the Yorkshire 'Rhubarb Triangle' during the nineteenth century and became a big industry. Rhubarb fool draws on this tradition. It is one of the simplest and best uses of the forced crop.
